segments.s350-g153: همی is a classical/progressive adverb meaning 'constantly' or 'continually' placed before the verb-like participle. آب‌داده is a participial/compound form ('water‑given', literally) used here as 'soaked/steeped' (a state), followed by two prepositional complements introduced by به (instrumental 'with'/'in'): به زهر 'with poison' and به خون 'with blood'. The conjunction و links the two instrumental phrases.
segments.s350-g154: به here functions adverbially/instrumentally ('with' or 'in the quality of') governing تیزی 'sharpness/keenness'. چو is a simile marker 'like/as', introducing الماس 'diamond' as the standard of comparison. The second coordinated phrase uses و to join رنگ 'color' qualified by the adjective/compound آب‌گون 'water‑colored' (i.e. pale/translucent), so the line attributes both sharpness and a water‑like hue.
segments.s350-g155: به فرمان is a prepositional phrase 'by the command'; یزدان is the genitive/patient of فرمان ('the command of God'). پیروزبخت is an epithet placed after this phrase ('fortunate' or 'blessed one') and functions as a vocative/epithet addressing someone—word order (preposition + noun + epithet) is typical in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s350-g156: نگون 'ill‑fated/accursed' precedes and qualifies the following clause. اندر is a locative/prepositional particle here introducing the act of hanging; آویزمش is an archaic/poetic form built around the root 'آویز' with the enclitic object ‑ش (= 'him/it'), functioning here as the verb-like phrase 'I hung him/it' (contracted poetic usage). بر درخت is the locative complement 'upon the tree' (بر = on). The sequence places the descriptive adjective and the hanging action together before the explicit location, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s350-g129: همی is a frequentative/habitual particle placed before the verb, giving a sense of continued or repeated action ('keeps saying'). گویدش is a fused form meaning 'he says to him' (گوید + ـش = to him). اژدهاگیر is a compound noun ('one who seizes/slays dragons') functioning as the complement of the imperative. باش is the second-person imperative 'be!' addressed to the addressee and links back to گویدش as quoted speech.
segments.s350-g130: گر introduces a conditional 'if'. از + خویشی marks the source/relationship ('from/through kinship with'); قیصر is the referent 'Caesar/emperor'. آژیر is a metaphorical noun 'alarm/bugle' here meaning 'an alarm/harbinger' and باش again is the imperative 'be!'; the whole clause gives a conditional instruction linked to the previous imperative.
segments.s350-g131: به پیش is a prepositional phrase 'before / in the presence of' (به + پیش). گرانمایگان is a plural noun meaning 'the illustrious/noble ones' and is the object of the prepositional phrase. روز و شب ('day and night') is a temporal phrase modifying the whole clause, placed after the prepositional phrase as is typical in Persian.
segments.s350-g132: بجز is a preposition meaning 'except'. نام is 'name' and here is the thing excepted. میرین is a proper name (Mīrīn) rather than the verb 'you die' given the context. نراند is a verbal form from راندن ('to drive/bring/put'); here it functions negatively in sense 'they put/placed/uttered nothing except…' (poetic usage), and به لب literally 'to the lip(s)' idiomatically means 'on their lips' (what they keep saying).
segments.s350-g133: هرانکس که is a correlative relative 'whoever / anyone who'. باشند is a third-person subjunctive/present form of 'to be' used here in the relative clause ('whoever is / may be'); زیبای is an adjectival/genitive construction 'of beauty' or 'beautiful', modifying بخت 'fortune' (so 'favored by fair fortune' or 'of beautiful fortune').
segments.s350-g134: بخواهد is third-person singular present 'he wants/wishes'. که introduces the content clause ('that'). ماند is an intransitive verb 'remain' (desiderative result wished for). بدو 'to him' marks the beneficiary/possessor. تاج و تخت are the two coordinated nouns 'crown and throne' that are the things hoped to remain; word order is verb → indirect object → direct object(s), common in older Persian.
segments.s350-g135: یکی here means 'a certain / there is one'. برز is an adjective 'high/exalted' qualifying کوهست ('is a mountain' or 'a mountain'), so the sequence reads 'a certain high mountain'. از ایدر نه دور is a prepositional phrase 'not far from here' (از = from, ایدر = here, نه دور = not far).
segments.s350-g136: همه جای is 'everywhere' or 'every place' (همه + جای). خوردن is a verbal noun/infinitive 'eating' used as a noun here ('places for eating'). گه is 'occasion / time' or 'place/time for', and کام here means 'satisfaction/pleasure'. و سور links 'pleasure' with 'sour' (سور = feast); together the phrase lists types of enjoyment associated with the mountain (eating, pleasure, and feasting). Word order lists place → activity → types of enjoyment.
segments.s350-g41: نشستنگهی is a compound of نشست (sitting) + گِه (place) meaning 'seat' or 'place to sit' and functions as the object. ساخت is the past tense (he made/built) and follows the object here (typical SOV order in older/poetic Persian). شایسته‌تر is the comparative adjective 'more fitting/appropriate' modifying نشستنگهی; it appears after the verb and qualifies the seat just created.
segments.s350-g42: برفت here is an archaic/petrified past-form meaning 'went away/left' (parallel to modern رفت); آنک is a relative/demonstrative 'those who' or 'which', followed by بودند (past plural 'were'), and بایسته‌تر is the comparative 'more fitting/proper'. The clause therefore reads 'those who were more fitting left.'
segments.s350-g43: به ایوان میرین is a locative phrase 'at/to the iwan (porch) of Mirin' (میرین as a proper name in postposition). نماندند is past plural 'did not remain/there was not left', and کس ('anyone/person') after the verb yields the sense 'no one remained' (negative existential word order).
segments.s350-g44: دو (not listed among the words here) quantifies مهتر: 'two elders/chiefs'. نشستند is the third-person plural past of 'نشستن' (they sat); the lemma/senses given in the data don't match this verbal use (so marked below). بر تخت is the prepositional phrase 'upon the throne'. بس is an adverb/intensifier here ('enough' / 'indeed' / 'simply') that follows to qualify the situation.
segments.s350-g45: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. میرین is the subject (proper name). بدیدش is the past verb بدید + enclitic object -ش 'saw him'. به بر درگرفت is a verbal phrase meaning 'seized/grasped him' or 'laid hold (upon him)'; درگرفت is a past action 'took hold / became involved' used transitively with the preceding directional particles.
segments.s350-g46: بپرسیدن here expresses the action 'to ask / asking' (the root notion 'ask'). مهتر is 'the elder/chief' as the agent. اندر گرفت is an idiomatic periphrasis: گرفت (took) with اندر ('in') forms 'began to' or 'took to (doing something)'. Together the phrase means 'the elder took up questioning' or more naturally 'the elder began to ask questions.'
segments.s350-g47: بدو 'to him' is the indirect-object phrase. گفت is the past 'said' (the lemma senses given in the data list refer to 'conversation' rather than the verb form, so that lemma sense does not match here). اهرن is a proper name or vocative; که introduces reported speech; با من بگوی is an imperative/subjunctive 'speak with me' (بگوی here is a hortative/subjunctive form meaning 'say/tell (to) me').
segments.s350-g48: ز هرچت literally 'about/from whatever you have' (ز = 'about/from', هرچت = 'whatever of yours'). بپرسم is the first-person present/subjunctive reading 'I will ask' / 'I shall ask' used to express the speaker's intention. بهانه مجوی is an imperative negative: بهانه = 'excuse', مجوی = 'do not seek' (negative jussive/imperative), so 'do not seek an excuse.'
segments.s350-g89: بیامد (past tense verb) + دوان (adverbial/participle) gives a single motion clause: “came running/rushed.” اهرن is a proper name standing after the verbal phrase (verb–modifier–name order common in poetry). چاره‌جوی is a compound noun (چاره + جو) functioning as an appositive to the name: “the seeker of remedy.”
segments.s350-g90: This is a directional phrase. به نزدیک = “toward / near”; هیشوی here functions as a proper name (not the literal adverbial sense found in dictionaries), and بنهاد is the past (perfect) of نهادن (to place/set) used with روی (face) to mean “turned/laid (his) face toward.” The sequence places the goal of the turning after the prepositional phrase.
segments.s350-g91: چو is the subordinating conjunction “when.” The following proper name اهرن is the subject, then a prepositional phrase به نزدیک دریا gives the place “near the sea,” and رسید is the past tense verb “arrived/reached.” Word order is typical of classical Persian: conjunction → subject → place phrase → verb.
segments.s350-g92: جهانجوی is a compound noun (جهان + جو) meaning an ambitious or world-seeking person and is the subject here. هیشوی again appears as a proper name (not the adverbial sense in lexicons). پیشین functions adverbially/attributively as “before / ahead / previous,” and دوید is the past tense verb “ran.” The order is subject (with epithets) + verb.
segments.s350-g93: ازو is a prepositional/relational clitic meaning “from him / of him.” بستد is the past form of بستن (here used transitively with نامه as its object) — in context it means he took/received or held the letter; آن نامه دلپسند is the noun phrase “that pleasing/pleasant letter.” Word order places the source (ازو) before the verb, then the object phrase.
segments.s350-g94: This is a two-part coordination. برو here is an enclitic/particle whose exact classical nuance is idiomatic (not the plain imperative “go”); آفرین is the exclamation/noun “praise” and کرد is the past auxiliary/verb “made / said” (so “he praised/exclaimed”). و = “and.” بگشاد is the past form built on گشودن “to open” (هغ: prefix ب- for past), and بند is “fastening/bond/seal” — together “and he opened the fastening/seal.”
segments.s350-g95: بدو = to him / unto him (prepositional clitic). گفت is the reported-speech verb “said,” though the provided lexical senses were nouns (here it is the past-tense verb). هیشوی is the speaker’s name (proper noun) following the verb — a common poetic order: indirect-object clitic → verb → speaker. کای is the vocative particle “O,” and راد مرد is a nominal phrase “generous / noble man” (راد qualifying مرد).
segments.s350-g96: This is the content of the speech. بیاید is the optative/modal present meaning “may/let (someone) come” or “come” in subordinate clause use; کنون = now; او = he; به is the preposition “to/toward”; کردار is a noun meaning “deed / conduct” and here denotes acting or the sphere of action; گرد is used adverbially/nominally with the sense “around / among / about” (so the phrase means roughly “come now to perform deeds among [us]”).
segments.s350-g137: This is an introductory noun phrase: 'یکی اژدها' = 'a certain/one dragon' (indefinite, narrative 'a certain'). The locative phrase 'بر سر تیغ کوه' follows the noun and means 'upon the head/top (سر) — the ridge or point (تیغ) — of the mountain (کوه)'; word order (determiner + noun + locative) is typical of Persian narrative style.
segments.s350-g138: This clause contains the verbal copula/predicate and the affected group: 'شده' (has become/stands), then the subject 'مردم روم' (the people of Rome). The small archaic element 'زو' = 'از او' (because of it / from it) gives the cause, and 'در ستوه' is the predicate complement meaning 'in distress/weary'. Word order here is slightly inverted (verb before subject), a common poetic feature.
segments.s350-g139: 'همی' marks continuous or habitual action ('keeps / continually'), 'ز آسمان' = 'from the sky' is the source phrase, and 'کرگس' is the subject (vulture). 'اندر کشد' is a poetic verb phrase meaning 'draws in / swoops down' (third-person singular). The verb after the locative and subject is normal in older poetic syntax.
segments.s350-g140: This parallel clause starts with 'ز دریا' = 'from the sea', followed by the subject 'نهنگ' (whale) qualified by the adjective/epithet 'دژم' (grim, sullen). The verb 'برکشد' uses the prefix بر- to indicate an upward or emergent motion ('rears up / lifts itself up').
segments.s350-g141: 'همی' again marks continuous action. 'دود زهرش' = 'the smoke of its poison' (زهرش = 'its poison/venom'), and 'بسوزد زمین' is a verbal clause meaning 'it burns/scorches the earth' (present/habitual sense). The verb form is classical/poetic and the possessive on 'زهر' is suffixal.
segments.s350-g142: 'نخواند' is the negative present (will not/does not utter/sing); its object/predicate is the exclamation 'آفرین' (praise/cheer). 'برین' is the fused locative/demonstrative 'بر + این' meaning 'upon this', so 'برین مرز و بوم' = 'upon this border and land'. The clause means no praise is spoken for this land — 'آفرین' is the word that would be uttered but is not.
segments.s350-g143: 'گر' introduces a conditional ('if'). 'آن کشته' = 'that slain one' (killed/slain as a noun phrase), 'آید' is the present/subjunctive/relative form 'comes', and 'به دست تو' = 'into your hand' (literally 'to your hand', meaning 'if you get it / if it comes into your power'). The final 'بر' is the preposition/prefix linking to the result clause that follows.
segments.s350-g144: 'شگفتی شوی' is the result clause: 'شوی' is second-person singular present of 'شدن' (to become) — 'you will become', and 'شگفتی' is a predicate adjective/noun 'a wonder / marvelous'. The locative adjunct 'در جهان سربسر' intensifies the result: 'throughout the world' or 'everywhere in the world' (سربسر = entirely/everywhere).
segments.s350-g113: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. تنگ اندر آمد is a compact clause where تنگ (narrow) + اندر (into/in) precede the past verb آمد 'came/entered' — typical Persian order adjective/locative → verb. پیاده دوان is an adverbial/participial phrase: پیاده 'on foot' modifies the action and precedes دوان (running/rushed), an archaic/poetic present‑participle/inflected form meaning 'ran/rushing'.
segments.s350-g114: پذیره شدش is a causative/passive construction meaning 'he was received / taken in' (پذیره from پذیرش), where شدش = 'happened to him / was done to him'. مرد روشن‌روان is the agent/identifier 'the bright‑souled man' (مرد = man/person; روشن‌روان a compound epithet). The whole segment states who received the incoming person.
segments.s350-g115: فرود آمد is a past verbal phrase 'alighted / dismounted' (فرود = descent; آمد = came). از باره جنگی is an adverbial/genitive phrase giving the cause or occasion: از ... باره 'on account of / because of the matter' and جنگی qualifies باره as 'of war / warlike'. سوار here is the subject/agent 'the rider/horseman' (in classical style the rider who has just dismounted).
segments.s350-g116: می‌و خوردنی خواست is a simple past clause: خواست 'wanted / requested' takes a direct object consisting of two coordinated nouns می‌و 'fruit' and خوردنی 'edible / food'. از نامدار marks the source/recipient 'from the famed one' (i.e., he asked the famed man for these things).
segments.s350-g117: یکی is an indefinite 'one/someone' introducing an agent. تیز here functions adverbially 'swift(ly)' modifying the verb. بگشاد is an archaic poetic past form of گشودن 'to open' (here: 'opened'). هیشوی is an adverb 'never' placed before لب 'lip/mouth' (هیشوی لب = 'with a mouth that never ...' or 'never before' in context); together the phrase pictures a sudden opening of the mouth.
segments.s350-g118: که introduces reported speech or a clause 'that / saying that'. شادان is an adjective 'joyful' describing the resulting state. بدی here functions as a verb ('you gave / you made') in the poem's diction, but that verbal sense is not listed in the provided senses (so returned as -1). نامور 'renowned' is the noun being affected; روز و شب 'day and night' give the time frame (standard coord. with و = 'and'). Grammatically this clause reports what was said or declared.
segments.s350-g119: نگه کن is an imperative 'look! / behold!' formed from نگه (gaze) + کن (imperative 'do'). بدین here is the demonstrative 'at this / upon this' (classical orthography separates it), followed by مرد 'man' and قیصر نژاد 'Caesar‑bred / of Caesar's lineage' (a genitive epithet). The sequence is an address commanding the listener to look at this particular man.
segments.s350-g120: که introduces an explanatory or relative clause 'for / that'. گردون گردان is a poetic compound: گردون 'the sky/heavens' and گردان 'revolving/turning' (together evoking fate/fortune in motion). بدو 'to him' is an enclitic prepositional form. گشت is the past of گشتن 'turned / became', and شاد is the predicate adjective 'happy / pleased' — together meaning the heavens/fortune turned favorable toward him.
segments.s350-g105: که introduces a reported clause ('that'); فردا بیاید is a subordinate time-clause with بیاید in the subjunctive/optative sense 'should/will come' ('he come tomorrow'). گو is an imperative/command 'say' placed before the name that follows; نامجوی is a proper name (vocative) being named by that imperative.
segments.s350-g106: بگویم is first-person singular (literary present/subjunctive or 'I will tell') and takes بدو ('to him') as its indirect object. هرچ گویی is a free relative 'whatever you say' where گویی is a 2nd-person present/subjunctive of 'to say'. The final بگوی is a jussive/imperative-like exhortation 'say it' (addressing the interlocutor), closing the instruction.
segments.s350-g107: به + [nouns] before the verb is used instrumentally/associatively here: به شمع آب دریا introduces the means/objects 'with candles and sea-water'. بیاراستند is a past-plural verb 'they adorned/arranged', agreeing with an implied plural subject.
segments.s350-g108: خورشها is plural 'the stews/dishes'. بخوردند is a past-plural verb here meaning 'they ate' (not 'struck'); و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'; می‌خواستند is past-continuous/intentional 'they wished/ intended' (about to do something).
segments.s350-g109: چنین 'thus' links to the foregoing narrative. تا سپیده is temporal 'until dawn'. ز is the poetic 'from/of', marking source or cause; یاقوت زرد is a compact poetic phrase 'yellow from (or of) ruby' (i.e. ruby‑tinted/yellowish effect), with adjective following noun in elevated word order.
segments.s350-g110: بزد is a simple past 'struck' or 'smote' used here for a sudden action of shining/striking. شید is a poetic epithet (the shining one / beloved) functioning as the agent (the sun/bright one). بر + شیشه لاژورد is the prepositional phrase 'upon the lapis (blue) glass', where لاژورد qualifies شیشه.
segments.s350-g111: پدید آمد is a compound verb 'appeared/ emerged' (past). از دشت 'from the plain' gives the origin; گرد here functions as 'dust' (i.e. dust‑covered) modifying سوار 'rider', so the phrase describes a rider appearing from the plain, covered with dust.
segments.s350-g112: ز دورش is a compressed phrase 'from afar' (دور + enclitic ش 'his/its' used idiomatically to indicate distance); بدید is the simple past 'he saw'; اهرن is a proper name (here rendered as a sighted person), and نامدار is an epithet 'renowned' modifying that name.
segments.s350-g65: برآریم is a hortative/subjunctive first-person-plural form ('let us raise' / 'we will raise') placed at the clause beginning. گرد here is the noun 'dust' (not the preposition 'around'); از سر is a prepositional phrase 'from/off the head of', with آن سوار 'that rider' as the genitive/possessed phrase. The verb governs the whole action: 'let us raise dust from the head of that rider.'
segments.s350-g66: نهان ماند is a two-word predicate meaning 'remained hidden' (نهان = hidden; ماند = remained, past of ماندن). این کار is the noun phrase 'this affair' (subject) that follows the verb in this classical Persian order, and یک روزگار is a temporal adverbial 'for a while / for a time'.
segments.s350-g67: به اهرن is the indirect object 'to Ahrn' (به + proper name). چنین گفت = 'thus he said' (چنین 'thus/so' + گفت 'said'). کز is a classical connective meaning 'that' (from که + از); کار گرگ is a possessive/construct phrase 'the wolf's deed' (کار = deed/affair, گرگ = wolf) and functions as the content of what he announces.
segments.s350-g68: بگویم is first-person singular present/subjunctive (here: 'I will tell' / 'I shall say') subordinate to what was said. چو functions as a temporal conjunction 'when/if', and سوگند یابم بزرگ is a small clause where یابم ('I obtain/find', subjunctive) takes سوگند ('oath') as its object, modified by بزرگ ('great/solemn'). Together: 'I will tell when I obtain a solemn oath.'
segments.s350-g69: که introduces a subordinate content clause 'that ...'. این کار 'this affair' is the topic; هرگز 'never' is an absolute negator placed before the eventual predicate; به‌روز و به شب ('by day and by night') are adverbial phrases giving 'day or night' — the verb of the clause appears in the next segment.
segments.s350-g70: نگویی is the negative-present/subjunctive form (ن + گویی) meaning 'you do not / you shall not say' (second person). نداری literally 'you do not have' + گشاده دو لب 'open two lips' is an idiomatic expression meaning 'you are not one who opens his lips' — i.e. you will keep silent. The second clause reinforces the prohibition on speaking.
segments.s350-g71: بخورد here is not literal 'eat' but the idiomatic past meaning 'befell'/'happened to' (it came upon someone). It governs the experiencer اهرن (Ahrn). آن سخت سوگند اوی = 'that severe oath of his', where سخت modifies سوگند and اوی is the enclitic third-person possessive 'his'.
segments.s350-g72: بپذرفت is past tense 'he accepted' (subject implied from prior clause). سرتاسر 'throughout/entirely' modifies the following object آن بند اوی — 'that whole bond of his' (بند here = bond/fetter/covenant). The verb + adverbial + object order is typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s350-g73: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. قرطاس را is the definite direct object (را marks the object) 'the sheet/paper'. جامه خامه کردن is a poetic idiom: literally 'to clothe (the paper) with the pen' — i.e. to write on the paper; کرد is the light verb 'did/made' completing that idiom in past tense.
segments.s350-g74: به marks the indirect object/dative 'to'. هیشوی here functions as an epithet/attribute attached to the proper name میرین (so the whole phrase names the addressee); although the provided lexical senses list it as an adverb, in context it behaves like part of a name. یکی نامه کرد is a simple clause 'wrote one/a letter' where یکی is an indefinite numeral/indefinite article 'one/a', نامه 'letter', and کرد again the past light verb 'did/wrote'.
segments.s350-g75: Initial که is a relative/complementizer introducing a description ('that / who'). اهرن is a proper name (here treated as the person being described). The second که again functions as a relative marker 'who', دهد/dارد is the present/historical-present 'has/possesses', and ز قیصر نژاد is a genitive phrase 'of Caesar's lineage' (ز = 'from/of', نژاد = 'lineage').
segments.s350-g76: جهانجوی is a descriptive noun 'world-seeker' (an epithet meaning ambitious/ambitious for the world). The repeated با ... با ... و ... lists possessions/attributes in parataxis: با گنج 'with treasure', با تخت 'with a throne', و داد 'and justice' — داد here is a noun 'justice' (not a past verb). The coordinating و links the last items.
segments.s350-g77: بخواهد is a 3rd-person form 'he wishes/wants' (here presenting desire). ز قیصر marks the source 'from Caesar'. همی is an archaic particle marking ongoing or emphatic aspect (common in classical Persian) placed near the verb; دختری is the indefinite object 'a daughter'. The verb precedes its object in this classical ordering.
segments.s350-g78: که is the relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing a qualifying clause. ماندست (compound of مانَد + ست) means 'remains / is left' (present perfective sense 'is remaining'). از دختران indicates the set 'of/among the daughters', and کهتری is an adjective 'lesser/inferior' — i.e. 'one of the lesser/younger daughters.'
segments.s350-g79: همی again marks habitual/continuous action 'continually/always'. اژدها is the subject 'the dragon'. دام اهرن کند is a classical word order where دام 'a trap' takes the object/goal, اهرن is the indirect object 'for Ahrn', and کند is the verb 'sets/does' — together 'the dragon sets a trap for Ahrn.'
segments.s350-g80: بکوشد is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive 'he strives/tries'. کزان is a compound/conjunctive adverb 'from which / by means of which' linking the previous clause to the purpose/result clause. بدنشان 'their bodies' is the possessed noun (بدن + third-person plural possessive), تن is a synonym 'body/self' used here idiomatically, and کند is the light verb again 'to make/do' — together expressing 'he strives so that, by that means, he may make their bodies (into) his own' (literal, slightly idiomatic).
segments.s350-g81: بیامد is a 3rd-person singular past tense verb 'came'. به نزدیک من is a locative prepositional phrase 'to/near me' with به + noun (نزدیک) + pronoun من as the goal; word order puts the locative before the subject. چاره‌جوی is the noun 'seeker of remedy' functioning as the subject/appositive that the verb refers to (poetic order places the noun after the locative).
segments.s350-g82: گذشته سخنها is a noun phrase 'past words/tales' with گذشته qualifying سخنها; in this poetic order the modifier precedes the plural noun. گشادم is a 1st-person past-form meaning 'I opened/revealed' (poetic variant of 'گشودم'), indicating the speaker's action. بدوی in the text here functions as an old/contracted form meaning 'to him' (i.e. 'to that seeker'), but none of the provided senses match that use so it is marked -1.
segments.s350-g83: ازان here means 'from that' or 'of that', linking the following nouns to what was just mentioned. گرگ و آن رزم دیده‌سوار is a coordinated noun phrase 'the wolf and that war‑rider' (دیده‌سوار = rider/horseman, a poetic compound), with آن marking the second noun 'that'.
segments.s350-g84: بگفتم is 1st-person past 'I said/told'. همه هرچ آمد به کار is an object phrase meaning 'everything/whatever came to use' (همه = all, هرچ = whatever; آمد به کار is an idiom 'came to use / proved useful'). The object follows the verb in this poetic line but is semantically what the speaker 'told'.
segments.s350-g85: چنان هم که introduces a comparison 'just as / so that'. کار مرا is 'my affair/task' (مرا = 'me' + را object marker split in the manuscript into م + را); کرد خوب is the past 'did (it) well' where کرد is 3rd‑person past and خوب qualifies the action. Note the poetic split of مرا into م + را; the provided sense for م does not match a clitic pronoun so it is marked -1.
segments.s350-g86: کند here is a 3rd-person modal/present form 'will/may do' (a jussive/impersonal sense 'will do'). بی‌گمان is an adverb 'undoubtedly' modifying the verb. کار این مرد خوب is the noun phrase 'this man's task/work (done) well' with خوب qualifying how it will be done; syntactically, کند governs the whole clause 'he will surely do this man's work well.'
segments.s350-g87: دو تن را is the accusative phrase 'two persons' (دو + تن) with را marking the definite/direct object. بدین here is the contracted prepositional phrase 'به + این' meaning 'by/with this' or 'at this'; the manuscript segmentation separates it into ب + دین, but دین as given does not match that function (so دین is marked -1) — the intended reading is 'بدین' = 'with/at this'. مرز مهتر کند: مرز 'border/frontier', مهتر 'chief/headman', کند 'makes/appoints' (3rd-person present/gnomic). The whole segment means 'he appoints two men as chiefs of this border.'
segments.s350-g88: چو is a comparative/temporal conjunction 'as/when'. خورشید را is the definite object 'the sun' with را. بر سر افسر کند is a locative-verb sequence 'places a helmet on (its) head' (بر = on, سر = head/top, افسر = helmet, کند = 'places/does'). The clause is a simile: 'as when he sets a helmet upon the sun's head.'
segments.s350-g9: به من ده کنون دختر کهترت is an imperative request: به + indirect object (من = “me”) + verb ده (give) is omitted here but understood with the following word order; کنون (now) is a temporal adverb placed before the direct object; دختر کهترت = “your younger/smaller daughter,” where کهتر + -ت is an archaic adjective with a 2nd‑person possessive suffix. Word order is standard Persian (to/for + recipient + time + object).
segments.s350-g10: به من تازه کن لشکر و افسرت is a coordinated imperative: به من (for me) marks the beneficiary, تازه کن is a two‑word imperative ‘make fresh/renew,’ and لشکر و افسرت (‘army and your officer/commander’) are the objects joined by و. کن is the imperative form of کردن 'do/make', and افسرت contains the possessive -ت (‘your’) attached to افسر.
segments.s350-g11: چنین داد پاسخ is a poetic SOV sequence: چنین (thus) + داد (gave) + پاسخ (answer/reply). The following که introduces the content (a reported question/utterance) and پیمان من = 'my covenant/pledge' (من here marks possession of پیمان).
segments.s350-g12: شنیدی مگر با جهانبان من is the reported speech clause. شنیدی is 2nd‑person perfect/past 'you heard / have you heard'; مگر here is a rhetorical/question tag ('isn't it?/have you not?'); با introduces the collaborator/companion 'with my جهانبان (world‑guardian)' and من again is the possessive sense. The clause therefore reads as a rhetorical question about whether the addressee heard the speaker's covenant with his guardian.
segments.s350-g13: که داماد نگزیند این دخترم is a subordinate clause introduced by که ('that/that/so that'). داماد (groom) is the subject; نگزیند is an archaic/poetic negative present/subjunctive form of گزین (choose) meaning 'shall not choose' or 'may not choose'; this is followed by the object این دخترم ('this daughter of mine', with -م = my).
segments.s350-g14: ز راه نیاکان خود نگذرم is a negative first‑person clause: ز = poetic 'from', راه نیاکان = 'the way/path of the ancestors', خود = 'one's own' (reflexive/possessive emphasis), and نگذرم is 1st‑person present/future negative 'I will not pass/leave/abandon.' Together it expresses refusal to depart from ancestral ways.
segments.s350-g15: چو میرین یکی کار بایدت کرد begins with چو ('when/if') introducing a conditional; میرین here is treated as a proper name (common in epic names), یکی کار = 'one/some deed', and بایدت کرد is a verbal cluster where کرد is the past tense 'did' and بایدت qualifies the action as a necessary/beneficial deed 'for you' (classical Persian allows such modal/benefactive clustering). Overall: 'If Mirin does a certain deed for you...'.
segments.s350-g16: ازان پس تو باشی ورا هم‌نبرد is the result clause: ازان پس ('from that/thereafter'), تو باشی is an optative/imperative sense 'you shall be / be you' (باشی here expresses becoming/standing in a role), ورا = 'to him' and هم‌نبرد = 'fellow‑warrior/comrade‑in‑arms.' The clause instructs that thereafter the addressee should be the other's comrade in battle.
segments.s350-g25: This is a compact, poetic prepositional phrase: ز (archaic form of از) + نزدیک = 'from near/nearby', followed by the proper noun قیصر 'Caesar'. The verb بیامد 'came' appears before the adverb برون 'forth/out', giving a slightly inverted, elevated word order common in epic poetry (V then adverb).
segments.s350-g26: دلش 'his heart' (possessive clitic ش) is the affected noun; زان سخن 'from that speech' gives the cause. کفته is a past-participial/adjectival use meaning 'crushed/stricken' and predicates the state of the heart. The trailing appositive جان پر زخون 'a life/full of wounds' further describes his condition; پر here is the adjectival 'full (of)'. There is no copula — a common poetic compression.
segments.s350-g27: به یاران 'to his companions' marks the indirect object; چنین گفت is the idiom 'he said thus' (past tense گفت). کان here functions as an archaic complementizer introducing the content of his speech ('that ...'). زخم گرگ is a noun phrase 'the wolf's wound' (genitive relation expressed by juxtaposition).
segments.s350-g28: نبد is an archaic negative/existential verb meaning 'there was not / does not exist'; it combines with جز 'except' to give the sense 'there is nothing except ...'. The prepositional phrase به شمشیر مردی سترگ 'by the sword of a mighty man' states the sole means; note the adjective/epithet سترگ follows مردی, a normal Persian adjectival order in poetry.
segments.s350-g29: ز میرین 'from Mirīn' gives the source/origin; میرین here is best read as a proper name. کی آید is an interrogative pattern ('who/when will come' — here 'who would come'), with آید in the imperfect/subjunctive sense 'would come' or 'will come'. چنین کارکرد 'perform such a deed' is the action being questioned; کارکرد is a verbal noun meaning 'the doing' or 'deed'.
segments.s350-g30: نداند is the past negative 'he did not know'. همی is an emphatic/continuative particle common in epic style (here strengthening the statement). قیصر is the subject (Caesar), which follows the verb in V–S order typical of older/poetic Persian. از introduces the object/topic 'about' and the doubled مرد مرد is an intensifying epithet meaning 'man of men' (i.e. a peerless or heroic man).
segments.s350-g31: شوم here is an optative/irrealis verb form ('may I...' or 'let me.../I should'), introducing the speaker's intention. زو 'from him' (archaic ו/ز + pronoun) marks the person to be asked. و functions as a connective ('then/and'), and بپرسم is the subjunctive 'I ask' (compressed sequence: 'let me ask him'). بگوید is a subjunctive/hortative form 'he may say' or 'he might answer'; مگر here is a modal particle meaning 'perhaps' or 'maybe' in this context. The line strings several subjunctive/optative verbs together — typical in hypothetical or deliberative speech.
segments.s350-g32: سخن با من 'a word/talk to me' (سخن with the preposition با + object من) = 'speak to me'. از introduces the topic 'about/concerning'. بی‌پی in the edition's token list looks anomalous (likely a corrupt or rare word); syntactically it is the topical noun governed by از. چاره‌گر is an adjectival/agentive noun 'one who finds a remedy' or 'remedial' and here serves to characterize the subject of the proposed talk ('about a remedy' / 'about someone who can cure').
segments.s350-g145: بدین عبارتِ قدیم، ازو = «از + او» functions as a possessive/relational pronoun meaning “of him / his.” یاورت is the noun ‘helper/ally’ with the enclitic -ت showing possession (your/his) not explicit here; پاک yzdān بود is the predicate: پاک یزدان = ‘the pure God’ and بود is the past copula ‘was’. Word order is noun phrase (possessor) + predicate (X was Y).
segments.s350-g146: به کامِ تو is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘at/for your desire’ (به = ‘to/for’, کام = ‘wish/desire’, تو = ‘you’). خورشید گردان is a nominal phrase where گردان is an adjectival/participial modifier ‘revolving’/‘turning’ modifying خورشید ‘the sun’. بود is the past copula ‘was’. The phrase means the turning sun was at your will.
segments.s350-g147: بدین here is the fused prepositional expression be + in (classical be + īn) meaning ‘by/with this’. زور و بالا = ‘strength (power) and highness (loftiness/status)’ joined with و. این دستبرد = ‘this seizure/raid/foray’ or ‘this act of plundering’. The whole segment lists the qualities/acts by which the speaker judges.
segments.s350-g148: ندانیم is the classical negative present/past form (ن + دانیم) meaning ‘we do not / we did not know’. همتای تو = ‘a peer/counterpart of you’. هیچ گرد is emphatic: هیچ = ‘no/none’ and گرد (here adverbial/locative) = ‘around/anywhere’ — together: ‘we know no equal anywhere.’
segments.s350-g149: بدو = ‘to him’ (به + او) indicating the indirect object of گفت (he said to him). گفت is the past verb ‘said’ (classical past). The clause then contains the imperative construction خنجری کن دراز: خنجری (‘a dagger’) + کن (imperative of کردن ‘do / make / draw’) + دراز (adjective ‘long’). Word order is object + imperative + adjective, a common terse poetic imperative.
segments.s350-g150: ازو = ‘of it / from it’ marking possession or origin. دسته here refers to the hilt/handle (contextually of the dagger) and بالاش = بالا + -ش (‘its top/above it’). چون = ‘like/as’ introduces a simile, and پنج باز = ‘five hawks’ (باز = hawk/falcon). Grammatically this is a descriptive phrase comparing the dagger’s upper part to five hawks.
segments.s350-g151: ز هر سوش = ز (from) + هر (each/every) + سو (direction/side) with the archaic locative ending, so ‘from every side’. برسان is an imperative jussive ‘bring/deliver’. دندان مار = ‘the snake’s teeth/fangs’ is the direct object. The command asks for fangs to be brought from all directions.
segments.s350-g152: سنانی here is a noun/adjective related to سنان (‘spear’) meaning ‘spear‑point / spearlike’ (or a proper name in some contexts) and is followed by the imperative بْرو ‘go’ (an imperative/command form). بسته = ‘bound/tied/fastened’ qualifies what follows; برسان is again ‘bring/deliver’. خار = ‘thorn’. Together the clause issues a sequence of commands: (attach) spear‑points, go, and bring the bound thorn.
segments.s350-g97: یکی here is the indefinite introducer 'a certain'; it precedes a string of epithets. نامداری is a noun used as an epithet 'renowned'. غریب و جوان are adjectival/descriptive words ('a stranger' / 'a young man') attached to that person; the sequence reads naturally as 'a certain renowned stranger and young (man)'.
segments.s350-g98: فدی کرد is a past action: فدی (sacrifice/ransom) + کرد (did/made) — 'offered himself as a sacrifice' (an idiomatic sacrificial/offering verb). بر پیش is a prepositional phrase 'in front of / before / in the presence of'. میرین روان functions as a compound proper name (Mirīn‑Ravān) rather than separate lexical items; thus it is the object of the preposition.
segments.s350-g99: کنون is a temporal adverb 'now'. چون here is the interrogative 'how' (rhetorical). کند is the verbal present/present‑general 'does / can do' used in the question. The verbal object phrase 'رزم نر اژدها' is a noun phrase 'the battle against the male dragon' (رزم = battle; نر modifies اژدها = 'male dragon').
segments.s350-g100: به چاره نیابد is a negated present statement: به + چاره 'a means/remedy' + نیابد (نیابَد) 'he does not find' (ن- negative prefix + یابد 'finds'). مگر زو رها = 'except from it (زو = از + او) release/freeing' — i.e. 'there is no solution except being freed from it.' Word order is slightly compressed/poetic but standard: object ('به چاره') before verb ('نیابد'), then the exception clause.
segments.s350-g101: مرا گفتن is an infinitival/object construction meaning 'to tell me' (مرا + گفتن), where را here marks the object (the data split shows م + را). The second clause کار بر دست اوست is a copular clause: کار 'the affair' + بر دست او 'in his hand' (i.e. under his control) + ست (poetic copula 'is'). The two clauses are coordinated by و. Overall: 'Tell me; the matter lies in his hands.'
segments.s350-g102: سخن گفتن is a gerund/infinitive noun phrase 'speaking' or 'to speak'. نیک qualifies it 'well / properly'. هرجا is an adverb 'wherever / wherever one is'. نکوست is an archaic/copulative predicative 'is good/fitting' (poetic form of نیکوست). Together: 'Speaking well is good everywhere.'
segments.s350-g103: تو امشب establishes the addressee and time 'you tonight'. بدین میزبان is a prepositional phrase 'for/to this host' (here written in two pieces in the token list); میزبان 'host' is the indirect object. رای کن is an imperative: رای 'decision/opinion' + کن 'do' → 'decide.' The whole is an imperative exhortation: 'Decide in favor of this host tonight.'
segments.s350-g104: بنه here is an archaic/poetic imperative 'place / set' (classical Persian verb form used in commands). شمع is the direct object 'a candle'. و links the two imperatives. دریا is used metaphorically as a large expanse/scene; دل‑آرای (literally 'heart‑adorning') is an adjective/compound meaning 'pleasing to the heart / charming'. کن is the repeated imperative 'do' applying to the preceding compound. The sequence commands: 'Set up a candle and make the scene heart‑pleasing.'
segments.s350-g121: هم at the start is a particle meaning “also/too.” از introduces the source or lineage (“from/of the seed of…”). قیصرانست is a fused form (قیصران + است) acting as the predicate “is of the Caesars” (the copula -est is attached). نیز at the end repeats/strengthens the sense of “also.” Word order is paratactic/poetic: source phrase + predicate + emphatic particle.
segments.s350-g122: همش here means “all of it / all his” and functions as a determiner for the following nouns. فر و نام are paired nouns meaning “glory and name/fame” (فر = royal/divine glory; نام = reputation). The second همش repeats “all of it” and scopes over گنج و چیز (“treasure and things/possessions”). The repeated و’s simply join the nouns in series.
segments.s350-g123: به دامادی is a prepositional phrase meaning “to the son-in-lawship / to becoming son-in-law.” قیصر specifies whose son-in-law. آمدش is آمد + enclitic -ش (“came to him”). رای is the notion/plan whose arrival is described: literally “to the Caesar’s son-in-lawship an opinion/plan came to him.” The order reflects an older/poetic Persian syntax (prepositional phrase + subject + verb with enclitic).
segments.s350-g124: همی is an archaic adverb meaning “still/indeed” placed before the verb to show ongoing desire. خواهد is the present/future of خواستن (“he wants/wishes”). اندر سخن (“in speech/in this matter”) specifies the sphere of consultation, and رهنمای is the noun “guide/adviser” that he wishes. Together: “he wants an adviser in the discussion/matter.”
segments.s350-g125: چنو (such/as) introduces a comparison; نیست negates (“there is not”). مر is an older form meaning “me” (object/pronoun referring to the speaker). قیصران را uses را as a topical/emphatic marker (“among the Caesars” as the domain under discussion). همال here functions figuratively as “peer/equal” (one who bears the same rank). The clause thus says, “There is no such equal among the Caesars as me.”
segments.s350-g126: جوانیست is the copular expression “(it) is youth / youth is (with him)” — i.e. youth accompanies him. با repeated before each noun is the preposition “with.” فر means royal/divine glory; برز means exaltation/highness; یال (“mane/crest”) is figurative for proud bearing or crest. The three qualities are listed as accompanying the youth.
segments.s350-g127: ازو is the enclitic prepositional pronoun “from him.” خواست is the past form of خواستن here in the sense “asked/requested.” یک‌بار (“once”) qualifies the action. و links to the second clause where پاسخ (“an answer/reply”) is the object and شنید is the past tense “he heard.” Word order is subject-less/elliptical and typical of epic diction: ‘From him he asked once, and (he) heard an answer.’
segments.s350-g128: کنون means “now/presently.” چاره (“remedy/means/plan”) plus دیگر (“another”) is the thing that has come; آمد پدید is a two-word verbal phrase meaning “came forth/appeared” (آمد = came, پدید = appear/manifest). The line states that now another solution/plan has come into being.
segments.s350-g17: به کوه سقیلا یکی اژدهاست is a locative opening: به + noun phrase gives the place (’at/on/to Mount Saqila’), followed by the existential phrase یکی اژدهاست (‘there is one dragon’). Here یکی functions as an indefinite/predicative ‘a certain / one’, and اژدهاست is the copula + noun (‘is a dragon’).
segments.s350-g18: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause (‘for/that’). کشور همه (‘the whole country/realm’) is the subject; پاک here is used adverbially/intensifying (‘entirely’ or ‘completely’), ازو marks source/agent (‘because of it’), در is the locative preposition ‘in’, and بلاست is the predicate noun (‘is calamity/affliction’ — archaic poetic form meaning the land is in trouble).
segments.s350-g19: اگر introduces a conditional clause (‘if’). کم کنی is a literary use of کردن with کم as the verbal idea ‘to lessen/dispatch/kill’ here (so کنی is the present/subjunctive 1st/2nd person marker for the conditional ‘if you …’). اژدها را is the definite object (را marks the direct object), and ز روم specifies origin/location (‘from Rum’).
segments.s350-g20: سپارم ترا is the main clause: سپارم is the 1st‑person present/subjunctive/optative form meaning ‘I will entrust/give’; ت is the clitic for the 2nd person object (‘to you’) and را reinforces the definite object; then a simple list of the things to be given follows (دختر و گنج و بوم = ‘daughter and treasure and realm’).
segments.s350-g21: که again introduces explanation/qualification (‘for/that’). همتای آن is a nominative phrase meaning ‘the equal/counterpart of that (one)’, followed by گرگ شیراوژنست where گرگ is the head noun and شیراوژنست is a descriptive (poetic/compound) adjective — the whole phrase says that there is a wolf whose equal is that (i.e. it is peerless or lion‑like).
segments.s350-g22: دمش زهر states a simple nominal clause: دمش (‘its breath/air’) + زهر (‘is poison’). The conjunction و links the next clause او دام آهرمنست, where او (‘it/he’) is the subject, دام (‘a snare/trap’) the predicate, and آهرمنست is a predicative attributing it to Ahriman (the evil spirit).
segments.s350-g23: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed phrase ‘thus gave an answer’ (چنین = thus; داد = gave — past tense; پاسخ = answer). The conjunction که introduces the content of the reply, and فرمان کنم uses کنم (present/subjunctive form) to express the speaker’s intention/resolve (‘that I shall command/order’).
segments.s350-g24: بدین آرزو is an adverbial phrase ‘by/with this wish’ (بدین = ‘with this’), followed by جان گروگان کنم where جان (‘life’) is the object and گروگان کنم (‘I will make [it] a hostage/pledge’) is the verb phrase — idiomatically ‘I will stake my life on this wish’ (کنم is the 1st‑person form of کردن used here as ‘I will do/make’).
segments.s350-g33: بشد تا is a classical introductory phrase meaning “so it came to pass that / it happened that”; تا here introduces the subordinate temporal/clausal idea (rendered “that/when”). به + ایوان میرین marks motion/toward the iwan (porch) of Mirīn — ایوان is the place noun and میرین is a proper name in postpositional position; چوگرد (an old poetic conjunction) reinforces the temporal sense “when/as.”
segments.s350-g34: پرستنده‌ای is an indefinite noun with the -ای suffix (“a worshipper / a certain devotee”). رفت is the simple past (“went”). و links the clauses (“and”), and آواز کرد is a light verb construction where آواز (voice/call) + کرد (did/made) = “cried out / raised his voice.” Word order is noun–verb then verb phrase describing the action.
segments.s350-g35: نشستنگهی داشت میرین is literally “Mirīn had a نشستنگه (seat/place).” داشت is the past auxiliary/verb meaning “had” here. که begins a relative clause describing that seat; ماه is the subject of that relative clause (“the moon”). The order places the possessed noun before the verb and then gives the possessor.
segments.s350-g36: This continues the relative clause: به گردون ندارد چنان جایگاه = “in/for the sky (گردون) does (ندارد) not have (چنان جایگاه) such a place.” ندارد is present/past-tense possession in classical narrative (“does not have / had not”), چنان qualifies جایگاه (“such a place”). The preposition به here indicates location/realm (“in the heavens”).
segments.s350-g37: جهانجوی is an epithet/noun “world-seeker / ambitious man” serving as the main subject. با introduces accompaniment (“with”), and گبر کنداوری is an appositive or descriptive phrase meaning “the gabr (Zoroastrian) named Kandauri” who accompanies him. This whole segment establishes the people present.
segments.s350-g38: یکی افسری = “a certain diadem/helmet” (یکی = a certain), followed by the locative phrase بر سرش (“on his head”: بر = on, سر = head, -ش = his). قیصری is an adjectival epithet “imperial,” post‑modifying the diadem. Word order is indefinite noun + object/location + adjectival qualifier.
segments.s350-g39: پرستنده گفت is the speech tag “the worshipper said.” Here گفت introduces reported speech; the following words اهرن پیلتن are a proper name or name phrase rendered as the content of his speech (the person who arrived). In classical texts such name compounds are often placed right after گفت to indicate what he announced.
segments.s350-g40: بیامد is the past tense verb “came.” به در is a fixed locative phrase “to the gate/door” (به + در). با یکی انجمن means “with a certain assembly/retinue” (با = with, یکی = a certain, انجمن = assembly/company). The whole phrase reports who arrived and with what company.
segments.s350-g49: This is an inverted, somewhat archaic copular construction: م + را (here combined as a fronted dative/topic phrase) indicates 'to me' or 'for me', followed by the noun آرزو 'desire/longing' and its predicate 'دختر قیصرست' (the daughter is of the Caesar). قیصرست is a fused copula (قیصر + ـست) meaning 'is (the) Caesar's / belongs to the Caesar' — so the whole clause reads literally 'To me there is a desire: the daughter of the Caesar.'
segments.s350-g50: کجا opens an adverbial clause 'wherever/where', followed by روم (Byzantium / Rome) marked by را (definite/partitive marker here), then سربسر 'all over/everywhere' and the predicate افسرست (afsar + ـست) 'is commander/leader' or 'is full of commanders'. Word order is adverbial (where) + place marked + quantifier + predicate, a poetic/node inversion common in older Persian.
segments.s350-g51: بگفتیم is a simple past/pluperfect plural 'we said' (بـ- prefix + گفتیم). و links clauses 'and'. پاسخ 'answer' is the object/topic, چنین 'thus/so' modifies داد; داد here is the past tense verb 'gave' (i.e., 'gave an answer'). باز 'again' modifies the giving/answering. The clause order (we said + and + answer thus gave again) is typical of narrative style.
segments.s350-g52: که is a complementizer 'that' introducing reported content. در کوه 'in/inside the mountain' sets the location. با 'with' introduces the opponent اژدها 'dragon'. رزم 'battle/war' + ساز (here used as a deverbal/nominal element meaning 'maker, preparer, means') together imply 'was making war / preparing to battle' — a compact poetic verbal noun phrase meaning 'was preparing for battle with the dragon in the mountain.'
segments.s350-g53: اگر 'if' introduces a conditional. بازگویی here is a gerund/masdar formed from بازگویى meaning 'recounting / repeating' and functions like 'if you tell/recount'. تو is the 2nd-person pronoun 'you'. آن کار 'that deed/affair' is the direct object, and گرگ 'wolf' qualifies which deed is meant ('the wolf's deed' or 'the deed concerning the wolf'). Word order is condition + verb-noun phrase + pronoun + object.
segments.s350-g54: بوی 'the scent' is the subject; مر here is an oblique/pronominal element ('of him' / archaic 'him/me' depending on context) and is followed by م + را which together function like an existential/copular sequence meaning 'is to me' or 'will be for me'. رهنمای 'guide' is the predicative noun and بزرگ 'great' is its adjective. The clause thus states '[the] scent of him will be a great guide for me.' Note the older word-division (م + را) reflecting classical Persian copula/oblique marking.
segments.s350-g55: چو is an archaic subordinating conjunction here meaning 'when'. بشنید is past tense 'he heard'. میرین is used as a proper name (Mīrīn) rather than the literal verb sense. ز + اهرن means 'from Ahrun' or 'of Ahrun' (z = 'from/of'), and سخن 'speech/words' is the object. The clause reads 'When Mirīn heard the words of Ahrun...'.
segments.s350-g56: بپژمرد is a past/predicative verb meaning 'withered/was wilted' (used figuratively for losing spirit or paling). و 'and' connects the two results. اندیشه 'thought' is the direct object; افگند is a past verb 'threw/cast' (here 'cast down' or 'laid low'), and بن is a noun 'root/foundation' used idiomatically (here roughly 'down'/'to the ground' or 'to the base of his mind'). The sequence describes a sudden collapse of spirit and a casting down of thought.
segments.s350-g57: 'که‌گر' is the poetic fused conditional 'که گر' = 'if / for if' introducing a reason or condition. 'کار' here means 'deed/affair' and is the thing being spoken of. 'آن نامدار جهان' is a descriptive noun phrase 'that renowned one of the world' (آن = that, نامدار = renowned, جهان = world), which together function as the possessor/agent of 'کار'. Word order follows classical Persian: determiner + epithet follow the head noun.
segments.s350-g58: 'به' marks the indirect object ('to Ahren'). 'اهرن' is a proper name (uncertain in modern reading) receiving the report. 'بگویم' is the first‑person present/subjunctive of گفتن used here in the sense 'I tell / I would tell' (speech‑frame verb that sets the clause). 'نماند نهان' is a verb + adjective/advocate: نماند (remain/ be left) + نهان (hidden) — together 'will not remain hidden'; in classical Persian the verb can follow the object and adjective phrase as here.
segments.s350-g59: 'سرمایه' is the subject noun meaning 'capital/ source/treasure' and is qualified by the genitive/attributive 'مردمی' ('of the people'). 'راستیست' is the copular predication (راستی + -ست) meaning 'is truth/ honesty' — so the clause states an identity: 'the people's capital is truth.'
segments.s350-g60: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'because of / from'. 'تاری' means 'darkness' (metaphorical obscurity) and 'کژی' means 'crookedness / unjust conduct'; they are joined by 'و' (and). 'بباید' is the impersonal/modal 'one must / it is proper to' and governs the following verbal form. 'گریست' is the present/infinitive meaning 'to weep / to lament' — together 'one ought to weep because of darkness and crookedness.'
segments.s350-g61: 'بگویم' here frames the reported remark ('I would say'). 'مگر' introduces the exception/conditional sense 'unless'. 'کان' in this context is the noun 'کَـن / کان' meaning 'mine, vein of ore' (classical sense) and is the object notionally linked to 'نبرده' ('has not borne/taken away'). 'نبرده' is a perfect/past negative form meaning 'had not taken/carried off'. 'سوار' is the agent 'the rider/horseman'. The clause thus expresses an exception: 'unless the rider had not taken the kan.'
segments.s350-g62: 'نهد' (from نهاد) is a present/optative third‑person singular 'places/sets'. 'اژدهار را' is the definite object 'the dragon' (را marks it). 'سر' (head) is the thing being placed and 'اندر کنار' is the locative phrase 'to the side / aside'. Together: 'he would put the dragon's head aside.' Note classical verb placement and the use of را to mark the direct object.
segments.s350-g63: 'چو' = 'when / since' introduces a temporal clause. 'اهرن' is the personal name. 'بود' is the past copula 'was'. The sequence 'مر مرا' is an archaic/dialectal set of datives/emphatic pronouns meaning 'to me / for me' (مر = to me; مرا used for emphasis), so the structure reads 'Ahren was to me a friend and backing.' 'یار و پشت' = 'friend and back/support', coordinated by 'و'.
segments.s350-g64: 'ندارد' = 'he/it does not have' (third person). 'مگر' here = 'except / save that'. 'باد' is the classical optative particle 'باد' meaning 'may / would that' (often used to express a wish or potential), so the clause reads parenthetically. 'دشمن به مشت' literally 'the enemy in the fist' — an idiom meaning the enemy is in (one's) grasp/hand. Put together: 'he had none, except (that) the enemy was in his fist.'
segments.s350-g1: ز + proper name (ز میرین) marks source/association: 'of/from Mirīn' or 'among Mirīn'. یکی بود is an existential construction ('there was one'); کهتر + به + سال is a comparative phrase where کهتر 'smaller/younger' takes به 'by' to mark the dimension (سال = years) — together 'younger in years'. Word order is archaic/classical (preposed source, then 'یکی بود').
segments.s350-g2: ز گردان رومی is again 'from/ out of the Roman troop/ranks' (ز + noun + adjective). برآورده here functions as a past/participial verb 'raised/provided' and takes یال ('crest, mane') as its object; the verb precedes the object in this terse, poetic order.
segments.s350-g3: گوی here is the verbal/quotative 'it is said' (literally 'they say'). بر منش = 'about/regarding his origin' (بر = on/about; منش = origin/lineage). نام او اهرنا places the noun phrase 'his name Ahrna' after the comment: 'they say (of his origin) his name is Ahrna.'
segments.s350-g4: ز تخم بزرگان = 'from the seed/stock of the great ones' (ز = from; تخم = seed/offspring; بزرگان = the great/forebears). رویین تنا is a compound adjective + noun 'iron-bodied' (رویین 'iron-hard' modifying تنا 'body'), placed after the genitive phrase to describe the forebears.
segments.s350-g5: فرستاد is the simple past 'he sent/dispatched'. نزدیک قیصر functions as a prepositional/locative phrase meaning 'to/near Caesar' (نزدیک here indicating proximity/presence with the ruler). پیام is the direct object 'a message' — together 'he sent a message to Caesar.'
segments.s350-g6: The first که introduces reported speech or purpose: 'that/so that'. دانی is the present/subjunctive form 'you know' or 'you should know' in reported speech (classical usage often reads as 'know that...'). The second که is the complementizer linking to the clause 'ما را نژادست و نام' — ما را (we + object marker) + نژادست 'is/are of lineage' + و نام 'and name' — roughly 'that you should know that we have lineage and a name.'
segments.s350-g7: ز میرین again marks association/source ('among the Mirīn' or 'of Mirīn'). به هر گوهری بگذرم: به + every + گوهری (jewel) and the verb بگذرم (first-person present/subjunctive form) here means 'I surpass/exceed' — a boastful assertion 'I exceed in every jewel.' The prefix ب‑ in classical Persian forms the present/subjunctive verbal form.
segments.s350-g8: به تیغ و به گنج درم repeats the preposition به for each item for emphasis: 'by sword and by treasure/dirhams'. برترم is the predicate 'I am superior' formed from برتر + the first-person suffix -م, so the clause literally reads 'I am superior.'
